questions.text: What is the fundamental unit of CPU utilization that forms the basis of multithreaded computer systems?
questions.explanation: According to the slide content, a thread is the fundamental unit of CPU utilization and forms the basis of multithreaded computer systems.

questions.text: In a modern multithreaded application, which of the following tasks within the application can be implemented by separate threads? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Modern applications use threads to handle multiple concurrent tasks such as UI updates, data fetching, spell checking, and processing network requests.

questions.text: Compared to process creation, thread creation is considered which of the following?

choices.label: More complex for kernels to manage
choices.text: More complex for kernels to manage

questions.explanation: The material notes that process creation is heavy-weight while thread creation is light-weight, leading to increased efficiency.

questions.text: Which of the following describes the multithreaded server architecture when a request arrives?

choices.label: The server spawns a new process to handle the request.
choices.text: The server spawns a new process to handle the request.

choices.label: The server blocks all incoming requests until the current one is finished.
choices.text: The server blocks all incoming requests until the current one is finished.

choices.label: The server creates a new thread to service the request and resumes listening for more requests.
choices.text: The server creates a new thread to service the request and resumes listening for more requests.

choices.label: The kernel shuts down the listening thread to prevent data races.
choices.text: The kernel shuts down the listening thread to prevent data races.

questions.explanation: Standard multithreaded server architecture involves creating a thread to service a specific request so the server can continue listening for additional incoming requests.

questions.text: What are the primary benefits of multithreaded programming? (Select all that apply)

choices.label: Responsiveness: Allowing continued execution if part of a process is blocked.
choices.text: Responsiveness: Allowing continued execution if part of a process is blocked.

choices.label: Resource Sharing: Threads share the memory and resources of the process naturally.
choices.text: Resource Sharing: Threads share the memory and resources of the process naturally.

choices.label: Economy: Lower overhead in creation and context switching compared to processes.
choices.text: Economy: Lower overhead in creation and context switching compared to processes.

choices.label: Scalability: Ability to utilize multiprocessor architectures.
choices.text: Scalability: Ability to utilize multiprocessor architectures.

questions.explanation: All these benefits are listed in the content as advantages of multithreading over traditional single-threaded process models.

questions.text: What are the specific challenges associated with multicore programming? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: The transition to multicore systems places pressure on programmers to handle activity division, balance, data splitting, dependencies, and complex debugging.

questions.text: Which term describes a system that supports more than one task making progress, even on a single processor core?
questions.explanation: Concurrency supports more than one task making progress (scheduling between them), whereas parallelism implies performing more than one task simultaneously.

questions.text: Which type of parallelism distributes subsets of the same data across multiple cores, performing the same operation on each?
questions.explanation: Data parallelism is defined as distributing subsets of the same data across multiple cores with the same operation applied to each subset.

questions.text: An Oracle SPARC T4 system with 8 cores and 8 hardware threads per core provides a total of how many hardware threads?
questions.explanation: 8 cores multiplied by 8 hardware threads per core equals 64 hardware threads.

questions.text: According to Amdahl's Law, if an application is 75% parallel and 25% serial, what is the speedup when moving from 1 core to 2 cores?
questions.explanation: Using the formula \( Speedup \le \frac{1}{S + \frac{1-S}{N}} \), where S is 0.25 and N is 2: \( \frac{1}{0.25 + 0.375} = \frac{1}{0.625} = 1.6 \).

questions.text: What does Amdahl's Law state about the serial portion of an application?

choices.label: The serial portion becomes irrelevant as N approaches infinity.
choices.text: The serial portion becomes irrelevant as N approaches infinity.

choices.label: The serial portion has a disproportionate effect on performance gains from adding more cores.
choices.text: The serial portion has a disproportionate effect on performance gains from adding more cores.

choices.label: Adding more cores will always reduce the serial portion of an application.
choices.text: Adding more cores will always reduce the serial portion of an application.

choices.label: The serial portion speedup is equal to \( 1/N \).
choices.text: The serial portion speedup is equal to \( 1/N \).

questions.explanation: Amdahl's Law emphasizes that the serial portion of an application limits the potential speedup, regardless of the number of cores added.

questions.text: Which of the following are examples of user-level thread libraries? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: POSIX Pthreads, Windows threads, and Java threads are the three primary user-level thread libraries mentioned. Kernel threads are supported by the OS itself.

questions.text: In the Many-to-One multithreading model, what happens if a single thread performs a blocking system call?

choices.label: The kernel creates a new thread to bypass the block.
choices.text: The kernel creates a new thread to bypass the block.

questions.explanation: In the Many-to-One model, many user threads map to a single kernel thread. If one thread blocks, the entire process blocks because only one thread can be in the kernel at a time.

questions.text: Which multithreading model is used by Windows and Linux?
questions.explanation: The One-to-One model is the standard for Windows and Linux, providing higher concurrency than the Many-to-One model.

questions.text: The Many-to-Many multithreading model is characterized by which of the following?

choices.label: Mapping many user-level threads to a single kernel thread.
choices.text: Mapping many user-level threads to a single kernel thread.

choices.label: Binding every user thread to a permanent kernel thread.
choices.text: Binding every user thread to a permanent kernel thread.

choices.label: Mapping many user-level threads to a smaller or equal number of kernel threads.
choices.text: Mapping many user-level threads to a smaller or equal number of kernel threads.

choices.label: Mapping exactly one user thread to one kernel thread.
choices.text: Mapping exactly one user thread to one kernel thread.

questions.explanation: The Many-to-Many model allows the OS to create a sufficient number of kernel threads for a large number of user threads.

questions.text: Which operating system historically utilized the Two-level multithreading model?
questions.explanation: The Two-level model, which is similar to Many-to-Many but allows binding a user thread to a kernel thread, was used by HP-UX, Tru64 UNIX, and Solaris 8 and earlier.

questions.text: What is Pthreads in the context of multithreading?

choices.label: A specific implementation of a thread library for Linux.
choices.text: A specific implementation of a thread library for Linux.

choices.label: An IEEE 1003.1c POSIX standard API for thread creation and synchronization.
choices.text: An IEEE 1003.1c POSIX standard API for thread creation and synchronization.

choices.label: A hardware-based threading mechanism for UNIX.
choices.text: A hardware-based threading mechanism for UNIX.

choices.label: A user-level library exclusive to Mac OS X.
choices.text: A user-level library exclusive to Mac OS X.

questions.explanation: Pthreads refers to the POSIX standard (IEEE 1003.1c) specification for thread behavior, which is widely implemented in UNIX systems.

questions.text: How can Java threads be created? (Select all that apply)

choices.label: By calling the `clone()` system call directly
choices.text: By calling the `clone()` system call directly

questions.explanation: Java threads are created by either extending the Thread class or implementing the Runnable interface; `clone()` is specific to Linux and `pthread_create` is POSIX.

questions.text: What are the primary methods explored for implicit threading? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: The slides explore Thread Pools, OpenMP, and Grand Central Dispatch (GCD) as strategies for implicit threading.

questions.text: What is an advantage of using a Thread Pool?

choices.label: It removes the need for synchronization in shared memory.
choices.text: It removes the need for synchronization in shared memory.

choices.label: Servicing a request with an existing thread is usually faster than creating a new one.
choices.text: Servicing a request with an existing thread is usually faster than creating a new one.

choices.label: It allows an infinite number of threads to be created.
choices.text: It allows an infinite number of threads to be created.

choices.label: It automatically converts data parallelism into task parallelism.
choices.text: It automatically converts data parallelism into task parallelism.

questions.explanation: One of the listed advantages of Thread Pools is that they provide faster service by reusing existing threads and allow the number of threads to be bounded.

questions.text: Which compiler directive is used in OpenMP to identify a block of code that can run in parallel?
questions.explanation: OpenMP uses `#pragma omp parallel` to identify parallel regions in C, C++, or FORTRAN code.

questions.text: In Apple's Grand Central Dispatch (GCD), what is the behavior of a 'concurrent' dispatch queue?

choices.label: Blocks are removed in LIFO order one at a time.
choices.text: Blocks are removed in LIFO order one at a time.

choices.label: Blocks are removed in FIFO order, but several may be removed at once.
choices.text: Blocks are removed in FIFO order, but several may be removed at once.

choices.label: It is a per-process queue called the 'main queue'.
choices.text: It is a per-process queue called the 'main queue'.

choices.label: It ensures that only one block is removed from the queue per second.
choices.text: It ensures that only one block is removed from the queue per second.

questions.explanation: GCD concurrent queues remove blocks in FIFO order but allow multiple blocks to be processed simultaneously depending on available threads.

questions.text: When a process invokes `exec()` after a `fork()` call in a multithreaded environment, what usually happens?

choices.label: Only the calling thread is replaced.
choices.text: Only the calling thread is replaced.

choices.label: The new program replaces the entire process, including all threads.
choices.text: The new program replaces the entire process, including all threads.

choices.label: The threads continue to run alongside the new program.
choices.text: The threads continue to run alongside the new program.

choices.label: The `exec()` call fails unless the process is single-threaded.
choices.text: The `exec()` call fails unless the process is single-threaded.

questions.explanation: As per the slides, `exec()` typically works by replacing the entire running process, including all of its threads, with the specified program.

questions.text: What are the common options for delivering a signal to a multithreaded process? (Select all that apply)

choices.label: Deliver the signal to the thread to which it applies.
choices.text: Deliver the signal to the thread to which it applies.

choices.label: Deliver the signal to every thread in the process.
choices.text: Deliver the signal to every thread in the process.

choices.label: Deliver the signal to certain threads in the process.
choices.text: Deliver the signal to certain threads in the process.

choices.label: Assign a specific thread to receive all signals.
choices.text: Assign a specific thread to receive all signals.

questions.explanation: All four are valid strategies for signal delivery in multithreaded systems depending on the signal type and OS implementation.

questions.text: What is the difference between asynchronous and deferred thread cancellation?

choices.label: Asynchronous terminates immediately; Deferred checks periodically at cancellation points.
choices.text: Asynchronous terminates immediately; Deferred checks periodically at cancellation points.

choices.label: Asynchronous is for kernel threads; Deferred is for user threads.
choices.text: Asynchronous is for kernel threads; Deferred is for user threads.

choices.label: Deferred terminates immediately; Asynchronous waits for the thread to exit.
choices.text: Deferred terminates immediately; Asynchronous waits for the thread to exit.

choices.label: They are identical in Pthreads but different in Windows threads.
choices.text: They are identical in Pthreads but different in Windows threads.

questions.explanation: Asynchronous cancellation terminates the target thread immediately, while deferred cancellation allows the thread to reach a 'cancellation point' before terminating.

questions.text: Thread-Local Storage (TLS) differs from local variables in that:

choices.label: TLS data is lost after a function returns.
choices.text: TLS data is lost after a function returns.

choices.label: TLS is shared among all threads in a pool.
choices.text: TLS is shared among all threads in a pool.

choices.label: TLS is visible across function invocations, similar to static data.
choices.text: TLS is visible across function invocations, similar to static data.

choices.label: Local variables are unique to each thread while TLS is not.
choices.text: Local variables are unique to each thread while TLS is not.

questions.explanation: TLS allows each thread to have its own unique copy of data that persists across multiple function calls, unlike local variables which only exist during a single invocation.

questions.text: In the context of scheduler activations, what is the role of the 'upcall' mechanism?

choices.label: It allows a user thread to override a kernel thread priority.
choices.text: It allows a user thread to override a kernel thread priority.

choices.label: It is a communication from the kernel to the upcall handler in the thread library.
choices.text: It is a communication from the kernel to the upcall handler in the thread library.

choices.label: It converts a One-to-One model into a Many-to-Many model.
choices.text: It converts a One-to-One model into a Many-to-Many model.

choices.label: It triggers an immediate context switch to a light-weight process.
choices.text: It triggers an immediate context switch to a light-weight process.

questions.explanation: Scheduler activations use upcalls as a communication mechanism from the kernel to the thread library to manage the number of kernel threads.

questions.text: What are the primary data structures of a Windows thread? (Select all that apply)
questions.explanation: Windows threads use ETHREAD, KTHREAD, and TEB. LWP is a concept used in Many-to-Many or Two-level models, not the standard Windows 1:1 implementation.

questions.text: How does Linux implement thread-like behavior via the `clone()` system call?

choices.label: It creates a complete copy of the parent process including the address space.
choices.text: It creates a complete copy of the parent process including the address space.

choices.label: It allows a child task to share the address space of the parent task.
choices.text: It allows a child task to share the address space of the parent task.

choices.label: It maps user threads directly to the hardware threads of the SPARC processor.
choices.text: It maps user threads directly to the hardware threads of the SPARC processor.

choices.label: It prevents any sharing of data structures between parent and child.
choices.text: It prevents any sharing of data structures between parent and child.

questions.explanation: Linux uses `clone()` to create tasks (threads) which can share or not share data structures (like address space) with the parent based on flag settings.
